1: 'Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help, relying on horses and trusting in chariots because there are so many and in cavalry because it is so strong — but who do not look to the Holy One of Israel or seek the LORD' — the parallel woe to Isaiah 30 against the Egypt alliance, now with explicit military-force analysis: 'hoi hayordim Mitsrayim le'ezra, woe to those going down to Egypt for help.' The military inventory: horses (sus, cavalry mounts), chariots (rekev, the 2-wheeled war machine that Egypt excelled in building — in the Battle of Kadesh/1274 BCE Egypt deployed thousands of chariots), cavalry/horsemen (parashim, the mounted riders). The contrast: 'veal-qedosh Yisrael lo shau, but they do not look to the Holy One of Israel' (shau from shaah = to look to, gaze upon) 'and the LORD they did not seek' (ve'et-YHWH lo darash, YHWH they did not consult = no prophetic inquiry, no priestly Urim and Thummim consultation).
2: 'Yet he too is wise and brings disaster; he does not take back his word. He will arise against the house of those who do evil and against the help of those who practice wickedness' — YHWH's wisdom superior to the political counselors: 'vegam-hu chakham vayave ra, and he also is wise and brings evil/disaster.' The 'he also is wise' (gam-hu chakham) is emphatic: the counselors who devised the Egypt strategy thought they were the wise ones — YHWH is also wise, and wiser. 'He does not take back his word' (velo hesir devaraiv, and his words he has not turned aside) = the prophetic word of judgment will not be recalled. 'He will arise against the house of those who do evil' (vaqam al-beit mere'im, and he will rise against the house of the evil-doers) and 'against the help of those who practice wickedness' (ve'al ezrat po'alei aven, against the help of those who work trouble/iniquity). Both the Judean court and the Egyptian ally are YHWH's targets.
3: 'The Egyptians are human, not God; their horses are flesh, not spirit. When the LORD stretches out his hand, the helper will stumble and the one being helped will fall, and they will all perish together' — the flesh/spirit anthropological contrast: 'veMitsrayim adam velo-el, and Egypt is a human being and not God.' The adam ('human being' from the earth, the mortal creature of Genesis 2:7) vs. el (God, the eternal power). 'Their horses are flesh and not spirit' (vesuseihem basar velo-ruach, their horses are flesh and not spirit) — the basar (flesh = the frail, mortal, perishable substance) vs. ruach (spirit = the divine energy that gives life, Genesis 2:7: 'breathed into his nostrils the breath of life'; Isaiah 6:3: 'my Spirit will not contend with humans forever, for they are mortal'). The flesh/spirit distinction that runs through Isaiah (Isaiah 31:3; Isaiah 40:6-7) becomes the Pauline theology of flesh vs. spirit (Romans 8:1-17; Galatians 5:16-25). 'When YHWH stretches out his hand' (uvatas YHWH yado, and YHWH stretches out his hand) = Exodus language (Exodus 3:20; Isaiah 7:5; Isaiah 9:15).
4: 'For this is what the LORD said to me: As a lion or a young lion growls over its prey, and though a whole company of shepherds is mustered against it, it is not frightened by their shouts or intimidated by the noise — so the LORD of hosts will come down to wage war on Mount Zion and on its hill' — the protective lion of Zion: 'ka'asher yehaggeh ha'aryeh vekhafir al-terfo, as the lion roars and the young lion over its prey' (haggeh = to growl/murmur over prey — the lion crouching over its kill and growling a warning). 'A whole company of shepherds is mustered against it' (yeqqare alaw melo ro'im, a fullness of shepherds is called against him) but the lion is 'not frightened' (lo-yechat) and 'not subdued' (lo-yishpal). The paradox: YHWH 'will come down to wage war ON Mount Zion' — coming down to fight at Zion's location, but as its defender, as the lion defending its prey (Jerusalem) from the shepherds (Assyrian commanders) trying to drive it away.
5: 'Like birds hovering over their young, so the LORD of hosts will shield Jerusalem; shielding, he will deliver it; passing over, he will rescue it' — the mother-bird protection and the Passover echo: 'ketsipporim afot ken yagen YHWH tseva'ot al-Yerushalayim, like birds hovering so YHWH of hosts will shield Jerusalem.' The tsipporim afot (birds hovering/flying over their nestlings) = the protective mother-bird imagery of Deuteronomy 32:11: 'like an eagle that stirs up its nest and hovers over its young, that spreads its wings to catch them and carries them aloft.' Psalms 91:4: 'He will cover you with his feathers and under his wings you will find refuge.' 'Passing over' (pesach) — the verb is unmistakably the Passover verb (Exodus 12:12-13, 23, 27: YHWH 'passed over' the houses of Israel in Egypt). Isaiah deliberately invokes Passover: the angel of destruction 'passed over' Israel; YHWH now 'passes over' Jerusalem, rescuing it from Assyria as he rescued Israel from Egypt.
6: 'Turn back to him from whom the people of Israel have so deeply defected' — the repentance call: 'shuvu livne Yisrael lasher amqu sara, return, O sons of Israel, to him from whom you have deeply defected.' The shuvu (turn/return = the repentance of Isaiah 30:15: 'in returning you shall be saved') combined with the magnitude of the departure: 'amqu sara, have deeply defected' (amaq = to go deep; sara = to turn aside/revolt; amqu sara = have gone very deep in apostasy). The deepness of the apostasy is matched by the depth of the invitation to return. The 'from whom' (asher amqu sara, from whom they deeply defected) = YHWH — the one they turned from deeply is the one they are invited to turn back to.
7: 'For in that day every person will throw away the idols of silver and gold that their own hands have made — things that have been their sin' — the idol-rejection in the day of YHWH: 'ki vayom hahu yim'asun ish eliley kaspo ve'eliley zahavo, for in that day each man will reject his silver idols and his gold idols.' The 'that their own hands have made' (asher asu lakhem yedeikhem chet, which your own hands made for you as sin) — the idols are the product of human hands (yad = hand, the same hand that made them can throw them away). 'Things that have been your sin' (chet = sin — the idols are themselves the embodiment of the sin, not merely its occasion). The same idol-rejection as Isaiah 2:20 ('on that day people will throw away to the moles and bats their idols of silver and idols of gold') and Isaiah 30:22 ('you will throw them away like a menstrual rag').
8: 'Then the Assyrian will fall by a sword that is not of any man; no human sword will consume him. He will flee from the sword, and his young warriors will be broken into forced labor' — the non-human sword of Assyrian defeat: 'venafal Ashshur becharev lo-ish, and Assyria will fall by a sword not of a man' (lo-ish = not of a man = not a human weapon). The emphatic point: Assyria's defeat will not be by human military action. 'Vecharev lo-adam tokkelenu, no sword of a human being will consume him' — two synonyms (lo-ish, lo-adam) both denying the human agency in Assyria's defeat. The fulfillment: 2 Kings 19:35/Isaiah 37:36: 'the angel of the LORD put to death 185,000 in the Assyrian camp' — not a human army. 'His young warriors will be broken into forced labor' (venosav lamas yihyeh, and his young men will become for tribute/forced labor) = the survivors enslaved.
9: 'His stronghold will crumble in terror, and his commanders will be terrified at the signal standard, declares the LORD, whose fire is in Zion and whose furnace is in Jerusalem' — the final defeat of Assyria's command structure: 'vesilao mippachad ya'avor, his rock/stronghold will pass away in terror' (sela = rock, the same word as YHWH the Rock in Isaiah 26:4 — Assyria's rock-stronghold crumbles in terror before YHWH the Rock). 'And his commanders will be terrified at the signal-standard' (vemissa'o mechita, from the signal he will be shattered) — the nessa/banner that Israel raises for YHWH's battle (Isaiah 11:10, 12; Isaiah 49:22; Isaiah 62:10) is the very thing that destroys Assyria's commanders. 'Whose fire is in Zion and whose furnace is in Jerusalem' (asher uro beTsiyon vetannuro biYerushalayim, whose fire is in Zion and his furnace in Jerusalem) = Jerusalem itself is the site of the divine fire (cf. Isaiah 33:14: 'who of us can dwell with the consuming fire?' = YHWH enthroned in Zion).
